request.question: 2002 animated film Groove Squad was one of many made-for-TV movies, such as Inspector Gadget and Dennis the Menace, on what series?

title: Inspector Gadget (film)
Inspector Gadget (1983 TV series)
Inspector Gadget: Mission 1 – Global Terror!
Inspector Gadget's Field Trip
Groove Squad
Inspector Gadget Saves Christmas
Gadget & the Gadgetinis
Inspector Gadget 2
Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons
Inspector Gadget
sentences: Inspector Gadget is a 1999 American action-comedy film directed by David Kellogg, written by Kerry Ehrin and Zak Penn from a story by Ehrin and Dana Olsen.
It is loosely based on the 1983 animated cartoon series of the same name.
It stars Matthew Broderick as the title character, along with Rupert Everett as Dr. Claw, Michelle Trachtenberg as Penny, and Dabney Coleman as Chief Quimby.
Three new characters were introduced, Dr. Brenda Bradford (played by Joely Fisher), Mayor Wilson (played by Cheri Oteri) and the Gadgetmobile (voiced by D. L. Hughley).
The film tells the story of how Inspector Gadget and Dr. Claw came to be in the cartoon.

Inspector Gadget is a French-Canadian-American animated comic science fiction television series that revolves around the adventures of a clumsy, dim-witted detective named Inspector Gadget—a human being with various bionic gadgets built into his body.
Gadget's nemesis is Dr. Claw, the leader of an evil organization known as "M.A.D." The series launched the "Inspector Gadget" franchise.
Inspector Gadget: Mission 1 – Global Terror!
is a graphic adventure game based on the animated television series "Inspector Gadget" and released for DOS in 1992.
In the game, Gadget is assigned a mission to rescue UN members who have been kidnapped and scattered around the world by his nemesis Dr. Claw.
Unlike other "Inspector Gadget" games, players do not have direct control over Gadget himself, but rather his niece Penny and their dog Brain, who must assist the clueless Gadget in his mission.

Inspector Gadget's Field Trip (onscreen title: Field Trip Starring Inspector Gadget) is a spin-off incarnation of Inspector Gadget, produced by DIC Entertainment in 1996 and aired on the The History Channel, where it was rerun until 2000.
26 episodes mixing animation with live-action were produced.
Don Adams returned as the voice of Inspector Gadget.

The series was an educational travelogue program for children, in which the animated Gadget would show viewers the many different sites in famous cities around the world via live-action-clips with historical facts.
The theme song is slightly similar to the one in the Gadget Boy series.
In fact, Gadget Boy sometimes makes a cameo appearance in the show (e.g. St. Augustine, Florida episode).
Groove Squad is a 2002 animated film which debuted as a part of Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons in late 2002.
The squad consists of three hip cheerleaders and the team mascot.
The Cheerleaders are accidentally turned into superheroes by a deranged genius.
They must defeat the power-hungry mad scientist while struggling to win the annual cheerleading competition.
Inspector Gadget Saves Christmas is a Christmas television special, featuring characters from the cartoon series "Inspector Gadget".
The special was produced by DIC Entertainment and LBS Communications, Inc., and aired on NBC on December 4, 1992.

Gadget & the Gadgetinis is a Canadian–French-Italian TV series spin-off of the 1983 series "Inspector Gadget", developed by DiC Entertainment in cooperation with Haim Saban's SIP Animation and released 2001–2003.
There are 52 episodes.
"Gadget & the Gadgetinis" is today the property of DHX Media, which holds the rights to most of DiC's library.

Inspector Gadget 2 (alternatively known as IG2, and credited as just Inspector 2 Gadget at the start credits) is a 2003 Australian-American live-action comedy film, released direct-to-video on VHS and DVD on March 11, 2003.
The film is based on the cartoon series created by DiC Entertainment and is a stand-alone sequel to the 1999 film "Inspector Gadget".
It was filmed in Brisbane, Queensland, Australia.
Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons was a series of animated made-for-TV movies, which lasted for just one season (2002) on Nickelodeon.
It started on October 6, 2002 and ended on December 29, 2002.

Produced by DIC Entertainment, the made-for-TV films are either based on various DIC productions such as "Inspector Gadget", "", and "Dennis the Menace", or are loose adaptations of stories from classic literature such as "Treasure Island" and "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea".
One film, "Groove Squad", which was about a group of crimefighting cheerleaders, was an original story.
All of these films were later released to VHS and DVD in the U.S. by MGM Home Entertainment under the "DIC Movie Toons" label.
In 2008, Gaiam re-released the films on DVD.
Inspector Gadget is a media franchise that began in 1983 with the DIC Entertainment animated television series, "Inspector Gadget".
Since the original series, there have been many spin-offs based on the show, including additional animated series, video games and films.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

hop2_docs: Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons | Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons was a series of animated made-for-TV movies, which lasted for just one season (2002) on Nickelodeon.  It started on October 6, 2002 and ended on December 29, 2002.  Produced by DIC Entertainment, the made-for-TV films are either based on various DIC productions such as "Inspector Gadget", "", and "Dennis the Menace", or are loose adaptations of stories from classic literature such as "Treasure Island" and "20,000 Leagues Under the Sea".  One film, "Groove Squad", which was about a group of crimefighting cheerleaders, was an original story.  All of these films were later released to VHS and DVD in the U.S. by MGM Home Entertainment under the "DIC Movie Toons" label.  In 2008, Gaiam re-released the films on DVD.

Groove Squad | Groove Squad is a 2002 animated film which debuted as a part of Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons in late 2002.  The squad consists of three hip cheerleaders and the team mascot.  The Cheerleaders are accidentally turned into superheroes by a deranged genius.  They must defeat the power-hungry mad scientist while struggling to win the annual cheerleading competition.

Sabrina: Friends Forever | Sabrina: Friends Forever (also known as Sabrina the Teenage Witch in Friends Forever) is a 2002 animated television film produced by DIC Entertainment and was first shown on "Nickelodeon Sunday Movie Toons" in the United States (however, in most other countries, it premiered on either Disney Channel or Toon Disney).  It is a continuation of the animated television series "" and was followed by another television series, "Sabrina's Secret Life".

The Wild Thornberrys Movie | The Wild Thornberrys Movie is a 2002 American animated adventure comedy-drama film based on "The Wild Thornberrys" television series.  The film follows Eliza Thornberry, on her quest to rescue a baby cheetah cub named Tally from ruthless poachers.  It was produced by Nickelodeon Movies and Klasky Csupo and distributed by Paramount Pictures.  The film released on December 20, 2002.  The film marks the third film made by Nickelodeon Movies to be based on a Nicktoon.

Tiny Toon Adventures 2: Montana's Movie Madness | Tiny Toon Adventures: Montana's Movie Madness is the second "Tiny Toon Adventures" game released on the Nintendo Game Boy.  It was released in 1993 and was developed and published by Konami.  It was released in Japan as "Tiny Toon Adventures 2: Buster Bunny no Kattobi Daibouken".

Albert (film) | Albert is a 2016 American computer-animated Christmas-themed musical comedy adventure TV movie produced by Nickelodeon and distributed by Paramount Television.  It premiered on December 9, 2016, and is Nickelodeon's first original animated TV movie.

Mary-Kate and Ashley in Action! | Mary-Kate and Ashley in Action!  is an animated television series featuring the voices and likeness of Mary-Kate and Ashley Olsen.  It is also a series of books that spun off, from the show.  The show premiered on October 20, 2001 on the ABC block Disney's One Saturday Morning, and was cancelled after one season due to negative ratings.  Reruns were later shown on Toon Disney and will be seen on Nickelodeon in 2016, as part of the network's purchasing of the television rights to Mary-Kate and Ashley's TV series and movies.
